server:
  port: 8086

spring:
  application:
    name: ingestion

  redis:
    # host and port will be overridden by Testcontainers automatically
    host: localhost
    port: 6379
    timeout: 15000

    # Redisson-specific configuration
    redisson:
      # Connection pool settings
      #
      # Maximum number of connections in Redis command connection pool.
      # Redisson doesn't distinguish max-idle, only cares about: maximum pool size and minimum keep-alive connections.
      #
      # Connection pool optimized for 24 stream consumers + business requests
      connection-pool-size: 96

      # Minimum number of idle connections to retain in the pool.
      # Even if Redis isn't accessed for some time, keep at least this many connections ready.
      # Helps avoid "cold start latency" — no need to establish a new connection when requests resume.
      #
      # High QPS scenario, pre-warm 24 connections.
      # Keep a batch of connections resident in the pool, aligned with consumer count.
      connection-minimum-idle-size: 24

      # Lettuce shares Pub/Sub and regular connections in the same pool (or a few independent).
      # But Redisson is different.
      #
      # Project currently doesn't use Pub/Sub.
      subscription-connection-pool-size: 8
      subscription-connection-minimum-idle-size: 2

      # Redisson thread model:
      #      nettyThreads:	IO threads
      #      threads:       	Redisson workers (locks/callbacks/scheduling)
      # These two thread pools are global, not per-bean
      #
      # Recommended empirical values:
      #      nettyThreads ≈ CPU * 2
      #      threads ≈ CPU
      netty-threads: 16
      threads: 8

      # Retry settings
      retry-attempts: 5
      retry-interval: 1000

  datasource:
    url: jdbc:mariadb://localhost/bsc?localSocket=/var/lib/mysql/mysql.sock&zeroDateTimeBehavior=convertToNull&useUnicode=true&characterEncoding=UTF-8&CharSet=UTF-8
    username: root
    password: ""
    driver-class-name: org.mariadb.jdbc.Driver

  jpa:
    hibernate:
      ddl-auto: validate
      naming:
        physical-strategy: org.hibernate.boot.model.naming.PhysicalNamingStrategyStandardImpl
    show-sql: false
    database-platform: org.hibernate.dialect.MariaDBDialect

logging:
  config: classpath:logback-spring.xml



# =====================================================
# NAC Stream consumer & producer configuration (used by RedisStreamConsumerBase)
# =====================================================
nac:
  stream:
    max-length: 200000            # Maximum number of messages to retain in stream
    trim-enabled: true
    trim-every-n-writes: 1000

    internal:
      poll-timeout-ms: 1000             # to avoid busy poll
      message-batch-size-per-poll: 20   # Too large batches will increase p95/max (worse tail latency).
      redis-consumer-count: 16
      worker-thread-pool-size: 32
    external:
      poll-timeout-ms: 1000             # to avoid busy poll
      message-batch-size-per-poll: 50
      redis-consumer-count: 8
      worker-thread-pool-size: 8

    consume-max-retry: 3              # Max retry attempts on message consumption failure
    create-group-include-history: true  # Include historical messages when creating group during tests
    pending:
      idle-threshold-ms: 10000        # Consider stuck if not ACKed within 10 seconds
      scan-interval-ms: 5000          # Scan pending messages every 5 seconds
      claim-batch-size: 10            # Number of messages to claim per XPENDING batch


# =====================================================
# Ingestion Controller Reactive Netty Config
# =====================================================
ingestion:
    recovery:
      enabled: true
      stale-threshold-seconds: 80
      fixed-delay-ms: 30000
      batch-size: 2000
      rate-limit-per-second: 100
      async:
        core-size: 4
        max-size: 16
        queue-capacity: 100
        keep-alive-seconds: 60
    reactive:
      thread-pool:
        core-size: 30
        max-size: 300
        queue-capacity: 10000
        keep-alive-seconds: 60

# =====================================================
# Metrics
# =====================================================
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        # /metrics is for: JVM / thread pools / custom metrics
        include: health,info,prometheus,metrics
  metrics:
    distribution:
      # Only enable histogram for lock-hold metric (not global),
      # so we can compute p95/p99 from *_bucket while keeping overhead low.
      percentiles-histogram:
        policy_reproc_lock_hold_seconds: true
      # Histogram bucket lower bound for fine-grained range.
      # Values below 1ms are still counted (not dropped), but won't be over-segmented.
      minimum-expected-value:
        policy_reproc_lock_hold_seconds: 1ms
      # Histogram bucket upper bound for fine-grained range.
      # Values above 60s are still counted (not dropped), and fall into top bucket(s)
      # instead of creating more detailed buckets at very large durations.
      maximum-expected-value:
        policy_reproc_lock_hold_seconds: 60s

# =====================================================
# Policy Reprocess Job Configuration
# =====================================================
policy:
  service:
    notification-url: http://policy-service/api/v1/entity-reset-notification
    connect-timeout-ms: 3000
    read-timeout-ms: 5000
  reprocess:
    # 1. producer side
    mark-init-batch-size: 1000

    # 2. cron job side
    scan-interval-ms: 30000
    scan-batch-size: 200
    max-candidates-per-scan: 2000
    scan-lock-ttl-seconds: 900
    rate-limit-per-second: 100

    # listener thread pool: handles PolicyReprocessRequestedEvent trigger
    listener:
      core-size: 1
      max-size: 2
      queue-capacity: 100
      keep-alive-seconds: 60

    # job thread pool: executes per-entity reprocess tasks inside each scan batch
    job:
      core-size: 8
      max-size: 16
      queue-capacity: 32
      keep-alive-seconds: 60

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0"
         xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0 https://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">
    <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

    <parent>
        <groupId>com.fortinet.nac</groupId>
        <artifactId>core_business_v2</artifactId>
        <version>0.0.1-SNAPSHOT</version>
    </parent>

    <artifactId>ingestion</artifactId>
    <name>ingestion</name>
    <description>ingestion</description>

    <dependencies>
        <!-- 1 Normal business logic dependency: only depends on the main code (src/main/java) of the common module, excluding any test code. -->
        <dependency>
            <groupId>com.fortinet.nac</groupId>
            <artifactId>common</artifactId>
        </dependency>

        <dependency>
            <groupId>com.fortinet.nac</groupId>
            <artifactId>classification</artifactId>
            <!-- Explicit version is required for classifier-based dependency resolution. -->
            <version>${project.version}</version>
            <!-- Depend on the attached plain library artifact, not the repackaged Boot main artifact. -->
            <classifier>plain</classifier>
            <exclusions>
                <exclusion>
                    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
                    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
                </exclusion>
            </exclusions>
        </dependency>

        <!-- Redisson dependency for direct Redis operations -->
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.redisson</groupId>
            <artifactId>redisson</artifactId>
        </dependency>

        <!-- 2 Used to depend on the test code of the common module -->
        <dependency>
            <groupId>com.fortinet.nac</groupId>
            <artifactId>common</artifactId>
            <version>${project.version}</version>
            <type>test-jar</type>
            <scope>test</scope>
        </dependency>

        <!-- The following 3 dependencies are for Redis/MariaDB testing against docker container in non-production environments -->
        <!-- Core Testcontainers -->
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.testcontainers</groupId>
            <artifactId>testcontainers</artifactId>
            <scope>test</scope>
        </dependency>
        <!-- Testcontainers + JUnit5 -->
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.testcontainers</groupId>
            <artifactId>junit-jupiter</artifactId>
            <scope>test</scope>
        </dependency>
        <!-- MariaDB Container -->
         <dependency>
            <groupId>org.testcontainers</groupId>
            <artifactId>mariadb</artifactId>
            <scope>test</scope>
        </dependency>

        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-data-jpa</artifactId>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.mariadb.jdbc</groupId>
            <artifactId>mariadb-java-client</artifactId>
            <scope>runtime</scope>
        </dependency>

        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-validation</artifactId>
        </dependency>

        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-devtools</artifactId>
            <!-- runtime: Not needed during compile time, but used during runtime, and not packaged in the production JAR -->
            <!-- Compile time: Your source code does not directly import classes from devtools, so it's not needed during compilation. -->
            <!-- Runtime: Spring Boot automatically detects and loads hot reload features from devtools during startup. -->
            <!-- runtime tells Maven:
                 "I don’t need to include its classes at compile time,
                  but I want it on the classpath at runtime." -->
            <scope>runtime</scope>
            <!-- optional: true means that if module A depends on current module, A won't inherit devtools; A must declare it explicitly to use. -->
            <optional>true</optional>
        </dependency>

        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.projectlombok</groupId>
            <artifactId>lombok</artifactId>
            <optional>true</optional>
        </dependency>

        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-test</artifactId>
            <!-- scope: test means it's only effective during testing (e.g., mvn test).
                 It will not be included in the final packaged JAR. -->
            <scope>test</scope>
        </dependency>

        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-webflux</artifactId>
        </dependency>

        <dependency>
            <groupId>commons-codec</groupId>
            <artifactId>commons-codec</artifactId>
        </dependency>

    </dependencies>

    <build>
        <finalName>ingestion</finalName>

        <plugins>
            <!-- Responsible for compiling Java source code -->
            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
                <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
            </plugin>

            <!-- Package the project into a JAR (with a main function); allows running the app via mvn spring-boot:run -->
            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
                <artifactId>spring-boot-maven-plugin</artifactId>
            </plugin>
        </plugins>
    </build>
</project>

The project has `spring-boot-starter-webflux` which means WebClient is available. This is actually a better choice than RestTemplate for non-blocking HTTP calls. Since WebFlux is already in the classpath, I can use WebClient for truly non-blocking I/O.

No Apache HttpClient dependency. But `spring-boot-starter-webflux` brings in Reactor Netty, which WebClient uses.

Now let me think about the design:

**Key insight about the user's proposal:**

The user says: "用 RestTemplate 配一个 300ms 的超时...这么短的时间不算阻塞吧？"


That's a misconception. RestTemplate is fundamentally synchronous and blocking—a short timeout just limits how long the thread blocks, not whether it blocks. A 300ms timeout still ties up a worker thread for that duration, and under load with only 32 worker threads, this could quickly exhaust the pool and cause cascading failures if PolicyService is slow.

Since WebFlux is already available, I should use WebClient instead for truly non-blocking I/O.

The handler will inject this client and call it after successfully updating the processing state, logging outcomes at debug and warn levels without propagating exceptions since these are best-effort notifications. I should also update the application.yml to reflect the 100ms and 200ms timeout values the user specified. I should update those timeout values to match what the user requested rather than keeping them separate. Now I'm planning out the implementation: I'll create a new config class for the policy notification settings, build a WebClient-based client to handle the actual HTTP calls, and modify the existing handler to use this new client instead of RestTemplate.

For the notification payload, I'm thinking about what makes sense to send—the entity ID and the new stage ("PENDING") should be sufficient for the receiving service to understand what changed. I'm also considering the necessary imports for setting up WebClient with Reactor Netty, including channel options for connection timeouts and the reactive HTTP connector.

On the timeout configuration, `responseTimeout` in Reactor Netty handles the full request-response cycle, which is simpler than managing separate read timeouts. For the connection timeout, I'll use `ChannelOption.CONNECT_TIMEOUT_MILLIS` with milliseconds as an integer value.

If PolicyService goes down, the notification will fail gracefully—the error gets logged but the handler continues and returns success, which aligns with the "best-effort notification" approach. Now I need to figure out where to place the WebClient configuration in the existing package structure, looking at how services and handlers are currently organized.

I'll create a `client` package for the HTTP client since this is specifically for external API communication. Writing the implementation... Writing the WebClient configuration...

Now I'm defining the PolicyNotificationClient component that injects the configured WebClient and notification URL, then implements a method to send entity reset notifications asynchronously with error handling and logging.

For the handler integration, I need to inject the notification client and call it after the state successfully updates to PENDING, but the timing relative to starting the state machine matters—I should notify immediately once the reset happens rather than waiting for the full state machine lifecycle to complete. is non-blocking since it uses WebClient with a subscribe pattern. Now I'm updating the configuration to tighten the timeout values—bringing the connect timeout down to 100ms and read timeout to 200ms to ensure the notification doesn't delay the main flow. The key insight here is that even though RestTemplate with a 300ms timeout sounds quick, it's still blocking and will tie up consumer threads, which is why the async WebClient approach is better.

## 关键问题：300ms 超时仍然是阻塞

`RestTemplate` 是**同步阻塞**的，不管超时设多短。300ms × 并发量仍会耗尽消费线程池（你有 32 个 worker 线程）。**超时只决定线程阻塞多久，不决定是否阻塞**。

server:
  port: 8086

spring:
  application:
    name: ingestion

  redis:
    # host and port will be overridden by Testcontainers automatically
    host: localhost
    port: 6379
    timeout: 15000

    # Redisson-specific configuration
    redisson:
      # Connection pool settings
      #
      # Maximum number of connections in Redis command connection pool.
      # Redisson doesn't distinguish max-idle, only cares about: maximum pool size and minimum keep-alive connections.
      #
      # Connection pool optimized for 24 stream consumers + business requests
      connection-pool-size: 96

      # Minimum number of idle connections to retain in the pool.
      # Even if Redis isn't accessed for some time, keep at least this many connections ready.
      # Helps avoid "cold start latency" — no need to establish a new connection when requests resume.
      #
      # High QPS scenario, pre-warm 24 connections.
      # Keep a batch of connections resident in the pool, aligned with consumer count.
      connection-minimum-idle-size: 24

      # Lettuce shares Pub/Sub and regular connections in the same pool (or a few independent).
      # But Redisson is different.
      #
      # Project currently doesn't use Pub/Sub.
      subscription-connection-pool-size: 8
      subscription-connection-minimum-idle-size: 2

      # Redisson thread model:
      #      nettyThreads:	IO threads
      #      threads:       	Redisson workers (locks/callbacks/scheduling)
      # These two thread pools are global, not per-bean
      #
      # Recommended empirical values:
      #      nettyThreads ≈ CPU * 2
      #      threads ≈ CPU
      netty-threads: 16
      threads: 8

      # Retry settings
      retry-attempts: 5
      retry-interval: 1000

  datasource:
    url: jdbc:mariadb://localhost/bsc?localSocket=/var/lib/mysql/mysql.sock&zeroDateTimeBehavior=convertToNull&useUnicode=true&characterEncoding=UTF-8&CharSet=UTF-8
    username: root
    password: ""
    driver-class-name: org.mariadb.jdbc.Driver

  jpa:
    hibernate:
      ddl-auto: validate
      naming:
        physical-strategy: org.hibernate.boot.model.naming.PhysicalNamingStrategyStandardImpl
    show-sql: false
    database-platform: org.hibernate.dialect.MariaDBDialect

logging:
  config: classpath:logback-spring.xml



# =====================================================
# NAC Stream consumer & producer configuration (used by RedisStreamConsumerBase)
# =====================================================
nac:
  stream:
    max-length: 200000            # Maximum number of messages to retain in stream
    trim-enabled: true
    trim-every-n-writes: 1000

    internal:
      poll-timeout-ms: 1000             # to avoid busy poll
      message-batch-size-per-poll: 20   # Too large batches will increase p95/max (worse tail latency).
      redis-consumer-count: 16
      worker-thread-pool-size: 32
    external:
      poll-timeout-ms: 1000             # to avoid busy poll
      message-batch-size-per-poll: 50
      redis-consumer-count: 8
      worker-thread-pool-size: 8

    consume-max-retry: 3              # Max retry attempts on message consumption failure
    create-group-include-history: true  # Include historical messages when creating group during tests
    pending:
      idle-threshold-ms: 10000        # Consider stuck if not ACKed within 10 seconds
      scan-interval-ms: 5000          # Scan pending messages every 5 seconds
      claim-batch-size: 10            # Number of messages to claim per XPENDING batch


# =====================================================
# Ingestion Controller Reactive Netty Config
# =====================================================
ingestion:
    recovery:
      enabled: true
      stale-threshold-seconds: 80
      fixed-delay-ms: 30000
      batch-size: 2000
      rate-limit-per-second: 100
      async:
        core-size: 4
        max-size: 16
        queue-capacity: 100
        keep-alive-seconds: 60
    reactive:
      thread-pool:
        core-size: 30
        max-size: 300
        queue-capacity: 10000
        keep-alive-seconds: 60

# =====================================================
# Metrics
# =====================================================
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        # /metrics is for: JVM / thread pools / custom metrics
        include: health,info,prometheus,metrics
  metrics:
    distribution:
      # Only enable histogram for lock-hold metric (not global),
      # so we can compute p95/p99 from *_bucket while keeping overhead low.
      percentiles-histogram:
        policy_reproc_lock_hold_seconds: true
      # Histogram bucket lower bound for fine-grained range.
      # Values below 1ms are still counted (not dropped), but won't be over-segmented.
      minimum-expected-value:
        policy_reproc_lock_hold_seconds: 1ms
      # Histogram bucket upper bound for fine-grained range.
      # Values above 60s are still counted (not dropped), and fall into top bucket(s)
      # instead of creating more detailed buckets at very large durations.
      maximum-expected-value:
        policy_reproc_lock_hold_seconds: 60s

# =====================================================
# Policy Reprocess Job Configuration
# =====================================================
policy:
  service:
    notification-url: http://policy-service/api/v1/entity-reset-notification
    connect-timeout-ms: 3000
    read-timeout-ms: 5000
  reprocess:
    # 1. producer side
    mark-init-batch-size: 1000

    # 2. cron job side
    scan-interval-ms: 30000
    scan-batch-size: 200
    max-candidates-per-scan: 2000
    scan-lock-ttl-seconds: 900
    rate-limit-per-second: 100

    # listener thread pool: handles PolicyReprocessRequestedEvent trigger
    listener:
      core-size: 1
      max-size: 2
      queue-capacity: 100
      keep-alive-seconds: 60

    # job thread pool: executes per-entity reprocess tasks inside each scan batch
    job:
      core-size: 8
      max-size: 16
      queue-capacity: 32
      keep-alive-seconds: 60
